FATHER PAYNE By Arthur Christopher Benson 1915 PREFACE Often as I have thought of my old friend "Father Payne," as we affectionately called him, I had somehow never intended to write about him, or if I did, it was "like as a dream when one awaketh," a vision that melted away at the touch of common life. Yet I always felt that his was one of those rich personalities well worth depicting, if the attitude and gesture with which he faced the world could be caught and fixed. The difficulty was that he was a man of ideas rather than of performance, suggestive rather than active: and the whole history of his experiment with life was evasive, and even to ordinary views fantastic. Besides, my own life has been a busy one, full of hard ordinary work: it was not until the war gave me, like many craftsmen, a most reluctant and unwelcome space of leisure, that I ever had the opportunity of considering the possibility of writing this book. I am too old to be a combatant, and too much of a specialist in literature to transmute my activities. I lately found myself with my professional occupations

### Plain English:
Book Project. FATHER PAYNE By Arthur Christopher Benson 1915 PREFACE I often thought about my old friend "Father Payne," which is what we called him affectionately. But I never really planned to write about him. Or if I did think about it, it felt like a dream that disappears when you wake up. Still, I always knew he was an interesting person worth writing about. The problem was catching and showing how he looked at the world and acted in it. He was someone who had lots of ideas but didn't do much. He liked to suggest things rather than actually do them. His whole life story was hard to pin down and seemed pretty weird to most people. Also, my own life has been really busy with regular work. It wasn't until the war happened that I finally had some free time, like a lot of other people. I was too old to fight, and I knew too much about literature to do anything else. I recently found myself with free time from my job.

both these aspects of the Northern capital which is altogether absent from the sparkling beauty of the city on the Isis, and from the genius of the place. The impression that Oxford makes upon those who, familiar with her from early years, have learnt to know and love her in later life is remarkable. Teeming with much that is ancient, she appears the embodiment of youth and beauty. Exquisite in line, sparkling with light and colour, she seems ever bright and young, while her sons fall into decay and perish. "Alma Mater!" they cry, and love her for her loveliness, till their dim eyes can look on her no more. And this is for the reason that the true lovableness of Oxford cannot be learnt at once. As her charms have grown from age to age, so their real appreciation is gradual. Not that she cannot catch the eye of one who sees her for the first time, and, smiling, hold him captive. This she can do now and then; but even so her new lover has yet to learn her preciousness. It is worth while to try to understand what are the charms that have grown with her growth.

### Plain English:
Both of these things are missing from the beautiful city on the Isis, and from its special character. Oxford has a remarkable effect on people who have known it since they were young and learned to love it as they got older. Even though Oxford is full of old things, it looks young and beautiful. It has beautiful buildings, bright light, and colorful views. It seems young and fresh while the people who studied there get older and die. "Dear Oxford!" they cry, and they love it for how beautiful it is, until they can't see it anymore with their old eyes. This happens because you can't understand why Oxford is so lovable right away. Just like its charms have grown over many years, it takes time to really appreciate them. That doesn't mean Oxford can't impress someone seeing it for the first time. Sometimes it can catch their eye and make them fall for it. But even then, this new lover still has to learn how special Oxford really is. It's worth trying to understand what makes Oxford so charming and why these charms have grown stronger over time.

"Well, I am not so certain about that, Ally; I don't go-in for magnanimity; and I believe there is just that touch of obstinacy in my nature, which would induce me to run counter to any proposition which was very hardly pressed. But when the suggestion was backed as it has been in this instance, I could not possibly doubt the sincerity of those who made it. And so, as you see, I am off." The place where the conversation, of which a fragment has just been given, occurred, was a broad gravelled path, the favourite promenade of such of the worthy townspeople of Southampton as prefer the beauties of nature to the attractions of the shops in the High-street. On one side was the broad water glistening in the bright, cheerful October sun, on the other a large strip of greensward fringed on the farther edge by a row of shining, white-faced lodging-houses and hotels. On the promenade, the grim cannons--trophies taken during the Russian war--were surrounded by happy children, fearlessly climbing upon the now innocuous engines

HOW WOOLLY BILLY CAME TO BRINE'S RIP I Jim's mother was a big cross-bred bitch, half Newfoundland and half bloodhound, belonging to Black Saunders, one of the hands at the Brine's Rip Mills. As the mills were always busy, Saunders was always busy, and it was no place for a dog to be around, among the screeching saws, the thumping, wet logs, and the spurting sawdust. So the big bitch, with fiery energy thrilling her veins and sinews and the restraint of a master's hand seldom exercised upon her, practically ran wild. Hunting on her own account in the deep wilderness which surrounded Brine's Rip Settlement, she became a deadly menace to every wild thing less formidable than a bear or a bull moose, till at last, in the early prime of her adventurous career, she was shot by an angry game warden for her depredations among the deer and the young caribou. Jim's father was a splendid and pedigreed specimen of the old English sheep-dog. From a litter of puppies of this uncommon parentage,

Man with the Dancing Bear 135 The Eagle 157 The Mule 179 Stripes the Unconcerned 199 JIM: THE STORY OF A BACKWOODS POLICE DOG I. HOW WOOLLY BILLY CAME TO BRINE'S RIP I Jim's mother was a big mixed-breed dog. She was half Newfoundland and half bloodhound. She belonged to Black Saunders, a worker at the Brine's Rip Mills. The mills were always running, so Saunders was always busy. It wasn't a good place for a dog. There were loud saws, heavy wet logs, and flying sawdust everywhere. These observations were made about three years ago, since which period, the spirit of architectural improvement has been fast extending from public buildings to individual mansions. Among the latter, the renovation or encasement of Apsley House, at Hyde Park Corner, with a fine stone front, is entitled to foremost notice. This splendid improvement is from the designs of Benjamin Wyatt, Esq. and is of the Palladian style. The basement story is rusticated, and the principal front has a handsome pediment supported by four columns of the Corinthian order. A bold cornice extends on all sides, which are decorated at the angles with Corinthian pilasters. The whole has an air of substantial elegance, and is in extremely good taste, if we except the door and window cases, which we are disposed to think rather too small. The Piccadilly front is enclosed with a rich bronzed palisade between leaved pillars, being in continuation of the classical taste of the entrance gates to Hyde Park, and the superb entrance to the Royal Gardens on the opposite

### Plain English:
A man wants to see his palace, but he's shown a plain brick house that's only forty or fifty feet wide instead. Someone wrote about this three years ago. Since then, people have been improving how buildings look. Both public buildings and private houses are getting better. Apsley House at Hyde Park Corner is a good example. It was given a new stone front. Benjamin Wyatt designed it. The style is called Palladian. The bottom level has a rough stone finish. The main front has a nice triangular roof piece held up by four tall columns. A thick stripe of stone runs around all the sides. Decorative stone pieces stick out at the corners. The whole thing looks fancy and well-made. But the doors and windows are probably too small. The front on Piccadilly Street has a fancy bronze fence between decorated posts. This matches the style of the gates to Hyde Park and the beautiful entrance to the Royal Gardens across the street.

# The Life and Adventures of Nicholas Nickleby by Charles Dickens

## Author's Preface

I started writing this story just a few months after finishing "Pickwick Papers." Back then, there were a lot of cheap schools in Yorkshire. There aren't many now.

For a long time, private schools showed how badly England was neglecting education. The government didn't seem to care that good education makes good citizens and happy people.

Here's the crazy part: Any man who was bad at every other job could open a school without any tests or training. But if you wanted to be a doctor, a pharmacist, a lawyer, a butcher, a baker, or a candlestick maker, you had to prove you knew what you were doing. Every single trade required training and qualifications. Except for teaching. Teachers didn't need to prove anything.

So it makes sense that schoolmasters were often complete idiots and fakes. That's what you'd expect when anyone could do the job.

On one side, ridge after ridge of mountains rolled away. They got smaller and smaller until they disappeared into the flat Sacramento Valley far in the distance. Down there it was already summer. The fields were hot and brown.

### Plain English:
William T. Adams, who wrote under the pen name "Oliver Optic," died before finishing his last book in the "Blue and Gray—On Land" series. This series was about his life's work writing stories for boys that became very famous in America. Many people asked for this final book to be finished and published. Edward Stratemeyer, who wrote the popular "Old Glory" series about the Spanish-American war, agreed to finish it. He took the story where Adams left off and completed it the way Adams probably wanted it to end. Stratemeyer did this work carefully and did it well. He kept the same style and feel as the rest of the series. The publishers want to honor Adams' memory. He had always stayed with this same publisher for many years. He was so loyal that he never gave any of his books to another publisher. This new book is the one hundred and eighth book published under the "Optic" name. It's great to report that because of how faithful Adams was to this publisher through good times and bad times, his books have sold more than two million copies.

For if to live well consist either in pleasure (pleasant passions, as the Epicureans taught), or in realizing one's own individual aim (the Stoic ideal), then this living well is, in either case, possible for animals, who can both enjoy pleasure, and accomplish their peculiar aim. Thus singing birds live a life desirable for them, if they enjoy pleasure, and sing conformably to their nature. If further we should define happiness as achieving the supreme purpose towards which nature aspires (the Stoic ideal), we should, even in this case, admit that animals share in happiness when they accomplish this supreme purpose. Then nature arouses in them no further desires, because their whole career is completed, and their life is filled from beginning to end. WHETHER PLANTS MAY BE TERMED HAPPY. Published in August 1912 by W. F. HAL Printing Company in Chicago. This book is dedicated to Nelly Kinzie Gordon.

PREFACE

There were two Fort Dearborns. The first one was built in 1803. American soldiers lived there until 1812, when Native Americans destroyed it right after a violent attack that happened that year. The second Fort Dearborn was built in the same place in 1816. It was used as a military post. But sometimes when the tribes around it were peaceful, the soldiers left for a while. In 1836, the military finally left the fort for good. This book tells the story of the first Fort Dearborn, also called the Old Fort Dearborn. The name "Chicago" was used to describe the river and the area around it for more than a hundred years before the first Fort Dearborn was built. It shows up on a map made by Franquelin in 1684 as "Chekagou." It appears in different spellings in written and printed records from that time and after. People have said that Chicago is the oldest Native American town in the West that we know about.

Early art in America was all about business. It followed the basic rule: make what people want to buy. Before cameras were invented, people wanted pictures of themselves. Americans were getting richer than their pioneer ancestors had been. Colonial leaders, rich landowners, and merchants wanted family portraits like the important people in Europe had. There weren't many artists to meet this demand. The artists that did exist weren't very good. The Colonial period didn't produce much quality art. It's surprising that without seeing good examples or having other artists to learn from, great artists like Copley in Boston and West in Pennsylvania appeared. A little later, Malbone in Newport created amazing small paintings that were better than anyone else's. When these men studied in Europe, their work got better and wider in style. But even their early work shows their real talent.

Preface.

The plan of this book should appeal to people's taste and judgment, no matter what they think of how it's done. The book is based on the idea of picking the most important events. It focuses mainly on the big events of American history. These events are treated as separate topics. In these ways, this book is different from other history books on the same subject. A book made this way has clear advantages. But it's worth noting that big events in history are like big things in nature and art. We like to look at the bold features of a country. We like to look at the biggest and most impressive buildings in cities. We like to look at the greatest achievements in art. In the same way, big events catch our attention and interest us because of how they connect to other things. They interest us because they show the greatest qualities of the human mind, perhaps.
